title: Pantelis Loupos
text:
University of California, Davis Graduate School of Management
I study how digital platforms create value, risk, and behavior through networks.
My research combines causal inference, machine learning, econometrics, and large-scale platform data to understand how users influence one another, how network structure predicts customer value, and how firms should design platform policies, recommendation systems, and data strategies.
Much of my work focuses on peer-to-peer and networked markets, including fintech platforms, social payment systems, crypto ecosystems, and digital communities. Across these settings, I ask how social connections, user activity, and algorithmic decisions shape adoption, engagement, fraud, demand allocation, and long-run platform growth.
I build empirical and analytical tools for problems where standard customer analytics is not enough because users are connected, behavior spills over across the network, and platform decisions affect both individual outcomes and system-level performance.
Research Publications CV

Research Publications CV

title: Pantelis Loupos
text:
Research Areas


Networked Digital Platforms
I study digital platforms where users are connected, behavior is visible, and value depends on social structure. My work examines peer-to-peer fintech platforms, social payment systems, digital communities, and crypto ecosystems to understand adoption, engagement, customer value, and platform risk.


Causal AI and Network Analytics
I develop and apply causal inference, machine learning, graph neural networks, and econometric methods for settings where users influence one another. A central theme in my work is how to estimate causal effects and predict behavior when outcomes are shaped by network spillovers, peer effects, and endogenous platform activity.


Platform Strategy and Decision Systems
I study how firms should use platform data to make better decisions about growth, retention, risk detection, recommendation ranking, and resource allocation. This stream connects academic research with practical problems in customer analytics, fintech, digital strategy, and AI-driven decision-making.

title: Pantelis Loupos
text:
Selected Publications and Current Papers

Fighting Fire with Fire: Infusing AI into Peer Review to Sustain Quality Scholarship. Joint work with Hemant Bhargava et al. Management Science, 2026.
Social Drug Dealing: How Peer-to-Peer Fintech Platforms Have Transformed Illicit Drug Markets. Joint work with Jörn Boehnke and Ying Gu. Annals of Operations Research, 2023. Article
What Reviews Foretell about Opening Weekend Box Office Revenue: The Harbinger of Failure Effect in the Movie Industry. Joint work with Yvette Peng, Sute Li, and Hao Hao. Marketing Letters, 2023. Article
Graph Neural Networks for Causal Inference Under Network Confounding. Joint work with Michael Leung. Revise and resubmit at The Review of Economic Studies. arXiv

title: Pantelis Loupos
text:
Teaching
I teach data-driven and AI-enabled decision-making across operations, customers, and analytics. In the MBA program, I teach core Operations Management and Customer Analytics. In the MSBA program, I teach Big Data Analytics, covering modern data systems, machine learning, deep learning, network analytics, Spark, Kafka, GNNs, and LLMs. I am also a certified NVIDIA instructor on Deep Learning.
Teaching

title: Research
text: My research examines digital platforms, social networks, social CRM, and business analytics. I am especially interested in how modern data infrastructure makes consumer and firm behavior observable in networked settings, and how methods from machine learning, econometrics, and network science can be used responsibly in those settings.

title: Research
section: AI, Digital Platforms, and Consumer Analytics
text:
AI, Digital Platforms, and Consumer Analytics
Digital platforms collect and act on large-scale behavioral data. My work in this area studies how platform data strategies shape technological, organizational, and economic choices, and how analytics can support consumer understanding without ignoring privacy and governance concerns.
Relevant work:

Platform Data Strategy
Fighting Fire with Fire: Infusing AI into Peer Review to Sustain Quality Scholarship

title: Research
text:
Social Networks and Peer-to-Peer Platforms
Peer-to-peer digital platforms create rich networks in which transactions, social connections, and offline interactions are intertwined. My research studies how network structure affects customer behavior, activity, acquisition, and valuation in these environments.
Relevant work:

The Structure and Evolution of an Offline Peer-to-Peer Financial Network
Network-Based Decision Support for Cold-Start User Valuation in P2P Platforms

title: Research
text:
FinTech, Crypto, and Blockchain Analytics
Digital financial platforms create new forms of transaction data and new managerial and regulatory questions. I study peer-to-peer payment networks, illicit activity on fintech platforms, and diffusion dynamics in crypto-token ecosystems.
Relevant work:

Social Drug Dealing: How Peer-to-Peer Fintech Platforms Have Transformed Illicit Drug Markets
Beyond Adoption: Active Participation and Recovery in Digital Market Diffusion

title: Research
section: Causal Inference and Machine Learning on Networks
text:
Causal Inference and Machine Learning on Networks
Networked data complicate causal inference because treatment, outcomes, and selection can all be shaped by peer effects and network structure. My current work studies how graph neural networks can help adjust for high-dimensional network confounding.
Relevant work:

Graph Neural Networks for Causal Inference Under Network Confounding

title: Research
text:
Retail Analytics and Recommendation Systems
I am also interested in ranking, attention allocation, and inventory dynamics in recommendation environments, especially where platform decisions interact with operational constraints.
Relevant work:

The Shadow Value of Attention: Recommendation Ranking with Inventory Dynamics

title: Teaching
text:
Program: Core MBA
Topics: Process analysis, capacity, queues, inventory, supply-demand matching, bottlenecks, quality, and operational tradeoffs.
This core MBA course teaches students how to analyze and improve the systems that produce and deliver goods and services. The course emphasizes operational decision-making under constraints, with applications to service operations, manufacturing, platforms, and supply chains.



Program: MBA
Topics: Customer segmentation, predictive modeling, customer lifetime value, acquisition, retention, churn, personalization, CRM strategy, and AI agents.
This course teaches students how to use customer-level data to make better marketing and growth decisions. The course connects statistical modeling, machine learning, and AI-enabled customer systems to managerial questions about which customers to acquire, retain, target, personalize for, and prioritize.



Program: Master of Business Analytics, San Francisco
Topics: Apache Spark, Kafka, machine learning, deep learning, graph neural networks, large language models, and generative AI.
This course teaches students how to work with large-scale data systems and modern AI methods in business analytics. The course combines scalable data engineering with machine learning applications in text, networks, prediction, and generative modeling.

title: Publications
text:
Hemant Bhargava et al. Management Science, 2026.
Examines how AI can support peer-review workflows as submission volume grows and reviewer capacity becomes increasingly scarce.



Jörn Boehnke, Pantelis Loupos, and Ying Gu. Annals of Operations Research, 2023.
Article | DOI
Uses Venmo data, text analytics, machine learning, and network analysis to detect illicit activity and characterize the financial networks behind digital drug dealing.
Media coverage: UC Davis News, Tech Times, Tech Xplore, Mirage News, EurekAlert!



Pantelis Loupos, Yvette Peng, Sute Li, and Hao Hao. Marketing Letters, 2023.
Article | DOI
Shows that some reviewers systematically act as harbingers of failure: their positive pre-release reviews predict weaker opening-weekend box office performance.
Media coverage: UC Davis, Science X / Phys.org, Mirage News, EurekAlert!, Daily Mail



Hemant K. Bhargava, Olivier Rubel, Elizabeth J. Altman, Ramnik Arora, Jörn Boehnke, Kaitlin Daniels, Timothy Derdenger, Bryan Kirschner, Darin LaFramboise, Pantelis Loupos, Geoffrey Parker, and Adithya Pattabhiramaiah. Marketing Letters, 2020.
SSRN | DOI
Develops a strategic framework for how digital platforms collect, govern, share, and monetize data across users, firms, and ecosystem partners.



Pantelis Loupos and Alexandros Nathan. Proceedings of the 10th Conference on Complex Networks, CompleNet 2019.
arXiv | DOI
Documents how Venmo’s peer-to-peer financial network forms and evolves, highlighting its connectivity, clustering, degree structure, and small-world properties.

title: Publications
text:
Revise and Resubmit

Graph Neural Networks for Causal Inference Under Network Confounding
Michael Leung and Pantelis Loupos. Revise and resubmit at The Review of Economic Studies.
arXiv
Develops a GNN-based framework for estimating causal effects in observational network data when treatment selection and outcomes are confounded by high-dimensional network structure.


Network-Based Decision Support for Cold-Start User Valuation in P2P Platforms: The Role of Initial Community Structure
Pantelis Loupos and Moran Cerf. Revise and resubmit, Decision Support Systems.
SSRN
Shows how a new user’s initial network position can predict future activity and customer value before meaningful behavioral history exists.

title: Publications
text:
Working Papers

The Shadow Value of Attention: Recommendation Ranking with Inventory Dynamics
Pantelis Loupos and Can Ürgün. To be submitted to Management Science.
Develops a multi-armed bandit framework for inventory-aware recommendation ranking, where exposure is allocated based on both demand potential and the shadow value of scarce inventory.


Beyond Adoption: Active Participation and Recovery in Digital Market Diffusion
Pantelis Loupos and Sriram Venkatesh. Working paper.
Develops a diffusion model for engagement-driven digital markets, showing how the interaction between cumulative adoption and active participation determines whether ecosystems scale, stall, or recover.
